elements.text: Captive audiences
They were built to instil terror. Now the giant domed prisons of the Netherlands are being reborn as arts hubs - with escape rooms. Charis McGowan reports

The bold nature of these works -some have commented on their frank confi guration and their "difcult brown-ish"palette- belies the challenging environment in which Grewal came to embrace painting. At Brighton's School of Art, he says, the emphasis was on satire and conceptual justifi cation: people doing bad paintings to make fun of the whole idea. His work, by contrast, was fi gurative an heavy on metaphor hinging on the expressive, the poetic, the dreamlike. "I might be too earnest," he says."There are different ways of making things and some are never going to be me. I'd much rather look at something that's gin o me  cy thn g especially in painting."

For all his love of charcoal, the Studio Voltaire exhibition will feature only paintings on canvas. The refreshed palette - the brilliant azures, yellows and pinks, not to mention the outsized dimensions of that central piece – will surprise fans as much as they have surprised the artist himself.

ne of the architectural features that marks out the skyline of Haarlem, a small Dutch city, is a 37.6m-high dome, crowning arotunda. You might assume it was built for religious purposes - until you notice the bars covering its 230 windows.

"My paintings often ref ect an emotional state" he says."Theye an expression ofa history, or memory -a kind of fi guring out. In the past, the dark layers have been directly confusing. They've been about hiding things. Those qualities are probably going to be in these new paintings now, but they're also bathein this ig, which surprised about." He ascribes this – and a "weird confi dence"-to time spent sketching around St Ives in Cornwall, as well as to arecent and widengi to Ini is  s the country his father comes from.

The foundation eventually got th cooperation of the Haarlem municipality, allowing them to acquire the 6.5m property andembark ona 25m renovation project. Without modifying the protected structure, they were permited to put extra windows in each cell, add a cinema and upgrade insulation and heating systems, slashing bills by 90%. It is now undergoing further development with a hotel, student accommodation and an escape room planned.

Operating as a prison from 1899until 2016, the Koepelgevangenis ("dome prison") is one of three panoptic penitentiaries built in the Netherlands. All were shut down in the past decade as part of the country's drive to reduce its prison population and are now being repurposed as arts venues.

Haarlem's Koepel opened as a cultural hubin 2022, thanksto a grassroots initiative ledby the local population. "So many of us Haarlemers had been biking around the dome for years, but no one could go in"says Jacqueline van de Sande, co-founder of the

After graduating, he worked as a cleaner, listening to homoerotic audiobooks while trying to keep his studio practice afloat. Then he did an MA at the Royal Drawing Schoolin London -and everything changed. He is keen to namecheck one tutor, Sarah Pickstone, for encouraging him to push his honesty to the limit."There was this energy that built up within me" he says."t demanded I take the clothes off he fi gures. There was still a lot of metaphor and symbolism, but now the fi gures were naked and in nature. From there, Igot looser and looser."

The Arnhem panopticon acquired Hospitality Group, is currently undergoing reconstruction with plans to transform the dome intoa multi-function even sace a hotel. Breda, jointly acquired by Being and VDD Project Development, is set to open in 2028. The revamped three-hectare site includes private housing around the do with the panopticon transformed into an exhibition space.

Grewal is often described as a queer fi gurative painter, with his south Asian heritage frequently added to the mix. None of these are labels he particularly covets."I'm qeenate ai he says."Idon't really know what else to say. t's almost like I'm being asked to label myself before I've made those personal discoveries myself. Ihaven't referenced being south Asian in a formal way in an exhibition -because that's not yet my experience."
Koepel Foundation, the organisation behind the building's transformation."We needed to open it up."
"igen sace.er can fi nd that in historiccity centres" says Bas van Dam, the founder of Being. "There's an obligation to give new life to places like this."
As you step inside, you're immediately struck by the vastness of the open space, bathed in sunlight streaming

They have already spoken to creative  including Belgian festival Tomorrowland. "Asa prison it used to be a space where yu didn't want to be and couldn't leave. We're going to turn that around"says van Dam. "You won't want to leave."

through the 16 original skylights. A cafe f lls the ground foor with chatter. Above, nearly all the former cells are being rented. There's a podcast studio, art school ateliers and gallery spaces, while the darkened cinema bar offers a more intimate charm.

He spent a month at Rhode Island Schoolof Design, on scholarship led by Gwen Strahle, who would talk to him about JMW Turner, Frank Auerbach and Leon Kossoff - artists who were fundamental to Grewal, yet unknown to his many of his peers. It felt like validation. He also discovered compressed charcoal and has not stopped using it since.

For a long time, it felt important that people knew that he ws from London. However, he has begun to wonder if the unspecifi ed locations in his paintings are his way of grappling with feelings of displacement."That is something t  esi eys."s lovely having people write about your work - but it's a funny thing when you're stil rying o fi gue t why you make it."

Back in Haarlem, the refurbishment has generated hundreds of jobs and the building welcomes up to 1,500 visitors daily. Yet if its multi-purpose atmosphere feels uplifting, ventures like prison escape rooms (a feature in both Haarlem and Arnhem) risk trivialising the building's past.

The Koepel's transformation is a far cry from the design's dark history. The panopticon dates back to the 17oos, f rst envisioned by British philosopher Jeremy Bentham. Circular prisons with a central guard tower meant prisoners could feel as if they were being watched, without really knowing whether they were or not.

During Covid, since museums were closed, Grewal tracked down National Gallery catalogues on eBay an se th wors he contained as drawing inspiration. He dived into queer cinema, became obsessed with Degas. " eall pe  ea int practice," he says."I was drenched init."

One of the most striking eperiences is historical one, in a tiny cell next to the main entrance. On the wall, panels describe the life of Hannie Schaf , one of the few female armed resistance fi ghters in the Netherlands. She was caught in March 1945 and imprisoned in the Koepel, before being executed aged 24.

He goes on:"In queer figuration, theresadesire for people to thin of adolescence, ofnymphs, of idealised youth. Theres a yearning for unattainable beauty, or purity, thata lot ofolder gay men have. Thee is nostalgiain y w, but there's also a darkness and a vulnerability. I'm trying to be as honest and as real as possible." Jake Grewal: Under the Same Sky is at Studio Voltaire, London, from 15 January until 13 April

Few panopticonic structures were ever built, but he Netherlands has eerges thnwitting"heartan" of panpticon design. The three well-preserved structures n Haarlem, Breda and Arnhem were engineered by Dutch architect Johan Frederik Metzelaar and his son Willem Cornelis, in the late 188os.

Sophie Poldermans, author of a book about Schaft's life, stresses its importance"So people are aware, not only of schaft's story but the fact it was a prison. You can still feel thenergyven thgitsnw ch lighter space." Nonetheless, as a Haarlemer herself, she is thrilled to see the dome gain a new life. "It's an icon.And it's beautiful."

After its closure as a prison, the Koepel was temporarily repurposed to house Syrian refugees while the government sought to sell the building. However, its bleak design, dark atmosphere and staggering heating costs of €400,ooo a year made it unappealing. "The

His studio is everything you could want an artist's studio to be. Paint pots and spills are everywhere. Books line the window sills and shelves, all within easy reach. When a British Museum curator recently discovered Grewal's charcoal drawings, she directed him to Georges Seurat. "Befoe Seutges topointillis he does his dissolving thing says Grewal opening a nearby book to show me. When I mention Eadweard Muybridge's studies of movement, he replies "Exactly!" and reaches for book about them."I haveall these clpoards he say lifting one up bearing a fragment of a andscape photograph, ready to be consulted while painting. "I think this is how Jenny Saville works. Like, I want to paint this place which is somewhere I haven't been, but which reminds me of Cornwall. And I want to paint it like Monet, in terms of a shimmery effect.I don't know how successful that will be but …."

It used to be that you couldn't leave. Now you won't want to
